items.myth: AI always means neural networks trained on big data.
items.reality: AI includes symbolic reasoning, search, planning, and smaller statistical models. Neural nets are one family—dominant in many products but not definitional.
items.example: A SAT solver or chess engine can be strong AI with zero gradient descent.

items.myth: If a system is labeled 'AI', it understands like a human.
items.reality: Narrow AI optimizes a defined objective on training or design-time knowledge; it does not automatically possess common sense, intent, or grounded world models.
items.example: A sentiment classifier outputs positive/negative scores—it does not 'feel' positivity.

items.myth: Generative AI is the same thing as artificial intelligence.
items.reality: Generative AI is a subset under deep learning, which sits under ML, which sits under AI. Many AI systems never generate content (e.g., fraud classifiers).
items.example: See What is Generative AI? for the Gen AI layer; this topic only places it in the hierarchy.
